framework: Canadian Aviation Regulations (CARs), Part IX — Remotely Piloted Aircraft Systems
scope: Public-safety Drone as First Responder (DFR), with emphasis on BVLOS operations
executiveSummary: Canada now permits a defined class of lower-risk BVLOS operations without an SFOC-RPAS. A public-safety agency can use this pathway only when the operation remains within the Level 1 Complex limits. The agency must operate under an RPAS Operator Certificate (RPOC), use appropriately declared equipment, and assign pilots holding the Level 1 Complex certificate. Urban or otherwise higher-complexity DFR missions generally remain outside this routine pathway and require an SFOC-RPAS supported by an operational risk assessment.

decisionRows.scenario: Small or medium RPA, BVLOS, uncontrolled airspace, 400 ft AGL or below, at least 1 km from a populated area
decisionRows.sfoc: No, if all Level 1 Complex conditions are met

decisionRows.scenario: Small RPA BVLOS over a sparsely populated area, or less than 1 km from a populated area
decisionRows.sfoc: No, if all Level 1 Complex conditions are met

decisionRows.scenario: Urban/populated DFR beyond the lower-risk limits
BVLOS in controlled airspace or an aerodrome environment
More than one RPA operated BVLOS at the same time
Sheltered operation with a small RPA within the prescribed building/structure envelope
decisionRows.sfoc: No, when conducted under the Advanced rules
decisionRows.route: Advanced pilot privileges; comply with sheltered-operation limits

decisionRows.scenario: EVLOS with trained visual observer, within 2 NM and in uncontrolled airspace
decisionRows.sfoc: No, when conducted under the Advanced rules
decisionRows.route: Advanced pilot privileges; comply with EVLOS limits

agencyNeeds: Organizational authority
An RPAS Operator Certificate (RPOC) for Level 1 Complex operations, or an SFOC-RPAS for operations outside that category.
Accountable executive
A named accountable executive responsible for the organization’s compliance and safety system.
Maintenance responsibility
A designated person responsible for maintenance, with equipment maintained in accordance with manufacturer instructions.
Operations governance
An operations manual, standard operating procedures, contingency/emergency procedures and documented operational control.
Training system
A training program and competence assurance appropriate to assigned crew duties.
Safety-risk management
A process to identify and manage operational safety risks; complex SFOCs require an operational risk assessment using Transport Canada guidance.
Qualified pilots
For routine lower-risk BVLOS, pilots need the Pilot Certificate — Level 1 Complex Operations.
Aircraft and supporting systems
The RPA and supporting systems must meet the applicable safety-assurance declaration standards for the intended operation.
Registration and records
Required aircraft registration, certificate access, maintenance records, crew records and operational documentation.
Airspace and site coordination
Confirm airspace classification, aerodrome distances, NOTAMs/restrictions, land access, local emergency coordination and privacy/legal constraints.
pilotPath: Pass the Advanced Operations online exam
Complete at least 20 hours of ground school through a flight school
Pass the Level 1 Complex online exam
Pass a Level 1 Complex flight review
Apply for the Pilot Certificate — Level 1 Complex Operations
lowerRiskLimits: More than 5 NM (9.3 km) from the centre of listed aerodromes
Small or medium RPA may operate at least 1 km from a populated area
Small RPA may operate over a sparsely populated area, or less than 1 km from a populated area, only with the applicable pre-validated declaration
Use an RPA and supporting systems with the required safety-assurance declarations
Operate under an RPOC with a Level 1 Complex pilot
dfrAssessment: Can a police/fire agency launch from a station or dock?

Potentially. The launch model itself is not the decisive issue; the operational category, airspace, population exposure, equipment declarations, supervision and procedures determine the approval route.
Can it fly BVLOS without an SFOC?
Yes, but only within the lower-risk Level 1 Complex envelope. Many urban DFR concepts will not fit that envelope.
Can it fly over a city to an emergency call?
Not as routine lower-risk BVLOS merely because it is public safety. Urban/populated BVLOS generally requires an SFOC-RPAS unless the operation fits a specifically permitted category.
Does emergency-response status remove the rules?

No general blanket exemption is identified in the public framework. Government emergency-response organizations are exempt from SFOC application fees, but still need the appropriate operating authority.
Can the agency operate in controlled airspace?
Level 1 Complex BVLOS is limited to uncontrolled airspace. Controlled-airspace BVLOS requires the appropriate SFOC route and coordination.
Can one pilot supervise several docked aircraft?
More than one RPA operated BVLOS at the same time is listed as a medium-complexity operation requiring an SFOC-RPAS.
Yes. The applicable technical declaration and operational-risk controls must address air-risk mitigation. More complex BVLOS applications are assessed through the SORA/ORA framework.
Yes, but scale depends on designing repeatable operations within an RPOC or obtaining an SFOC whose conditions support the intended sites, airspace and mission set.
